explicit_text: I was born at home in Pasadena, California, and grew up there before the war. My father worked as a gardener in Pasadena, serving many families for years, and my mother was a stay-at-home mom who sewed for us. We lived in a rented house, and our landlord was kind enough to let us store our belongings when we were forced to leave during the war.
implicit_text: I was born at home in a city in Southern California known for its cultural institutions and rose bowl, and grew up there before the war. My father worked as a gardener in that city, serving many families for years, and my mother was a stay-at-home mom who sewed for us. We lived in a rented house, and our landlord was kind enough to let us store our belongings when we were forced to leave during the war.

explicit_text: During the summers before the war, my family and I enjoyed going to the beach, and one of the few beaches we were allowed to visit was Redondo Beach. We would set up a big tent there and spend the day by the ocean. It was a special part of my childhood weekends along with visits to relatives.

explicit_text: San Pedro was another beach area where my family went during the summers. We also had relatives living there, including my uncle who lived in a three-story house. Unfortunately, during the war, the FBI accused my uncle of signaling Japanese ships from his porch, and he was taken away, which was a frightening experience for our family.

explicit_text: Both of my parents were Issei from Ehime Prefecture on the island of Shikoku in Japan. My father came to the U.S. first in 1919 and later returned to Japan to marry my mother in an arranged marriage. They both came from charming seaside towns, and they wished to be buried back in Japan, which I have visited to pay respects at their graves.

explicit_text: My father first came to the United States in 1919 and worked in Spokane, Washington, on the railroads. He earned enough money there to return to Japan and marry my mother. Spokane was the place where he started his life in America before settling in California.

explicit_text: Before the war, my family and I would go to the Zen temple in Little Tokyo, Los Angeles, especially for Japanese school. It was a cultural and community center for us, and I remember passing through Los Angeles and seeing Union Station on the way. Little Tokyo was an important part of our Japanese American identity.
implicit_text: Before the war, my family and I would go to the Zen temple in the historic Japanese American district of downtown Los Angeles, especially for Japanese school. It was a cultural and community center for us, and I remember passing through downtown Los Angeles and seeing the main train station on the way. That neighborhood was an important part of our Japanese American identity.

explicit_text: I don't remember the exact moment Pearl Harbor was attacked, but my father told me about it often. On December 7, 1941, my sister told him the news while he was working in the yard, and at first, he couldn't believe it. He was shocked that Japan would attack America, a country he respected, and he sensed that our lives would change drastically after that day.
implicit_text: I don't remember the exact moment the naval base in Hawaii was attacked, but my father told me about it often. On December 7, 1941, my sister told him the news while he was working in the yard, and at first, he couldn't believe it. He was shocked that Japan would attack America, a country he respected, and he sensed that our lives would change drastically after that day.

explicit_text: My father felt that the people in the United States Army did not fully understand how great and large America was compared to tiny Japan. After Pearl Harbor, he thought that the Army's perspective might have influenced the backlash against Japanese Americans. The Army was part of the broader wartime context that affected our family.
implicit_text: My father felt that the people in the U.S. military did not fully understand how great and large America was compared to tiny Japan. After the attack on the naval base, he thought that the military's perspective might have influenced the backlash against Japanese Americans. The Army was part of the broader wartime context that affected our family.

explicit_text: My father believed that the people in the Imperial Japanese Navy had a better understanding of the world and were less likely to have done something like the Pearl Harbor attack. He was totally shocked by the attack and thought the Navy knew more about the consequences. This distinction was part of his reflections during that time.
implicit_text: My father believed that the people in Japan’s naval forces had a better understanding of the world and were less likely to have done something like the attack on the naval base in Hawaii. He was totally shocked by the attack and thought the Navy knew more about the consequences. This distinction was part of his reflections during that time.

explicit_text: When Executive Order 9066 was issued, our family knew something was coming. My mother began sewing duffle bags and heavy bathrobes for us because there were rumors we would be sent to cold or desert places. We prepared as best we could, and my father arranged to store our belongings safely with friends and neighbors before we were forced to leave.
implicit_text: When the presidential order authorizing the removal of Japanese Americans was issued, our family knew something was coming. My mother began sewing duffle bags and heavy bathrobes for us because there were rumors we would be sent to cold or desert places. We prepared as best we could, and my father arranged to store our belongings safely with friends and neighbors before we were forced to leave.

explicit_text: The FBI took my uncle away from his home in San Pedro, accusing him of signaling Japanese ships from his porch. This was a frightening and traumatic event for our family, and it happened before we were sent to the assembly center. The FBI's actions were part of the broader suspicion and surveillance of Japanese Americans during the war.
implicit_text: The federal investigative agency took my uncle away from his home in the Los Angeles port district, accusing him of signaling Japanese ships from his porch. This was a frightening and traumatic event for our family, and it happened before we were sent to the assembly center. Their actions were part of the broader suspicion and surveillance of Japanese Americans during the war.

explicit_text: Our family was sent to the Tulare Assembly Center, which was located on a fairground next to California State Route 99. I remember the fence and being told not to get close because guards might shoot. We lived in barracks, and I had a bad reaction to the smallpox and typhoid shots given to us upon arrival, which made me cling to my mother for comfort.

I remember the fence and being told not to get close because guards might shoot. We lived in barracks, and I had a bad reaction to the smallpox and typhoid shots given to us upon arrival, which made me cling to my mother for comfort.

explicit_text: The Tulare Assembly Center where we were held was right next to California State Route 99, and I remember seeing cars whizzing by on the highway. It was strange to be confined so close to a busy road but unable to leave. That highway was a constant reminder of the outside world while we were detained.

explicit_text: In September 1942, we were transferred from Tulare to the Gila River War Relocation Center in Arizona. The camp was in a desert area near Phoenix, and when we arrived, it wasn't quite ready. I remember walking carefully across planks over open ditches to get to the bathroom because I was too scared to cross alone. Despite the hardships, I found moments of camaraderie with other children and was excited to start school again.
implicit_text: In September 1942, we were transferred from the assembly center in California to a relocation center in Arizona’s desert near the state capital. The camp was in a desert area near Phoenix, and when we arrived, it wasn't quite ready. I remember walking carefully across planks over open ditches to get to the bathroom because I was too scared to cross alone. Despite the hardships, I found moments of camaraderie with other children and was excited to start school again.

explicit_text: On the train trip from Tulare to Gila River, we stopped at Union Station in Los Angeles. I recognized it because my family often passed by there when going from Pasadena to Little Tokyo. I wanted to peek out the window but had to keep the shades down because soldiers with rifles were patrolling. It was a tense and surreal moment for me as a child.

The area was very desolate and desert-like, very different from what Phoenix is today. My parents and I didn't know what to expect, and the camp was not fully prepared, which made it a frightening experience for me as a young child.

explicit_text: Eleanor Roosevelt was not directly mentioned in my personal story, but I know from history that she was an important figure during the war. Her advocacy helped bring attention to the injustices faced by Japanese Americans, including those like my family who were forcibly relocated. Her influence gave hope to many in our community during those difficult times.
